source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: The metaverse is inevitable: a majority of people will spend a majority of waking hours in virtual worlds within our lifetimes.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the metaverse — defined as virtual worlds where people spend the majority of their waking hours — is inevitable within our lifetimes, framing this as a straightforward linear extrapolation of observable trends in consumer time allocation rather than a speculative leap. He supports this view by pointing to the trajectory of video games, which have grown from under 5% to a majority of combined UK video game, music, and movie sales over roughly 20 years, demonstrating a sustained and accelerating shift in how people spend leisure time. A key structural driver he identifies is that video games are the only major entertainment format that benefits from Moore's Law, meaning they continuously improve in quality and immersion over time in a way that movies and music fundamentally cannot. The investment implication is a long-term structural tailwind for companies building virtual worlds, gaming platforms, and enabling technologies, though Baker does not name specific beneficiaries in this segment of the transcript. No specific price targets, timeframes beyond 'within our lifetimes,' or company names are cited.
detail: This is a linear extrapolation of current trends in consumer time spent. Video games have gone from sub-5% to a majority of combined UK video game/music/movie sales over 20 years. Video games are the only entertainment format riding Moore's Law, making them continuously better unlike movies or music.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses the word 'inevitable' and 'semi-inevitable' multiple times; frames as linear extrapolation of observable data.
quote_fragment: inevitable that a majority of people will spend a majority of waking hours in virtual worlds
